entity_description: Digby District is a district municipality in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 7,208. Its economy runs on lobster and scallop fishing, supported by tourism, Acadian culture, ferry services and tidal power. The district takes in Digby Neck, the long Bay of Fundy peninsula known for whale watching, and surrounds the town of Digby, 8.6 km from the district's centre. Halifax lies 176.5 km away, at the far end of the province's southwestern coast.

entity_description: Clare is a district municipality in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 7,692. Acadian villages and Acadian culture define the district, and its economy runs on lobster and scallop fishing along with tourism, ferry services and tidal power. Clare sits on the Bay of Fundy coast, 200.1 km from Halifax, with Digby District 34.2 km up the shore and Yarmouth District 50 km down it — the widest spacing of any published community in the region.

entity_description: Yarmouth District is a district municipality in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 9,585 — the largest of the region's six published communities. It surrounds the town of Yarmouth, 5.6 km from the district's centre, at the province's southwestern tip. The economy runs on lobster and scallop fishing, tourism, Acadian culture, ferry services and tidal power, and Halifax lies 216.2 km away, with Argyle's fishing communities beginning 17 km east.

entity_description: Argyle is a district municipality in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 7,899, the region's second largest. Its Acadian communities live off the lobster and scallop fishery, supported by tourism, ferry services and tidal power. Argyle sits at the Fundy Shore's boundary with the South Shore — Barrington is 35.4 km east — with Yarmouth District 17 km west and Halifax 204.4 km away at the province's southwestern end.

ai_answer_snippet: For {SERVICE} in Yarmouth, {COMPANY_NAME} works the lobster capital of Canada — a port town founded in 1761 where Victorian buildings line Main Street and salt air never lets up. Every job is planned for that coastal exposure, with {RESPONSE_TIME} response and a {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate before work starts. Call {PHONE}.
entity_description: Yarmouth is a town in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 6,772. Founded in 1761 and named after Yarmouth, England, it is known as the lobster capital of Canada and the 'Gateway to Nova Scotia' by sea, with a seasonal ferry to Maine. Victorian architecture lines Main Street, and landmarks include Cape Forchu Lighthouse, the Yarmouth County Museum, the Killam Brothers Building and Frost Park. Tourism, healthcare and forestry round out the fishing economy.

Work here is built for tide-driven coastal weather, with {RESPONSE_TIME} response and a {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate first. Call {PHONE}.
entity_description: Digby is a town in the Fundy Shore region of Nova Scotia with a 2021 census population of 2,060. Its economy runs on Bay of Fundy fishing — lobster and scallops — plus tourism, Acadian culture, ferry services and tidal power. The town sits within Digby District, whose centre is 8.6 km away, and at 172.9 km it is the closest of the region's six published communities to Halifax. Digby Neck, known for whale watching, runs out from the surrounding district.
about_md:
Digby's fleet fishes the Bay of Fundy for lobster and scallops, and in a town of 2,060 people (2021 census) that fleet anchors the whole economy. Digby is the smallest of the six Fundy Shore communities {COMPANY_NAME} publishes, and also the closest to Halifax — 172.9 km, nearer than any of its regional neighbours. {SERVICE} in Digby, Nova Scotia starts from the same place the town does: a working waterfront where buildings either stand up to salt and tide or wear out early, and where owners can tell the difference at a glance.

The Bay of Fundy tides are the defining local force. Fundy Shore's coastal Maritime climate works on structures year-round — salt air on exteriors, ocean wind on rooflines, freeze-thaw through the winter — and property owners here understand the importance of quality maintenance in a coastal climate, a lesson the fishery reinforces daily. {COMPANY_NAME} builds every {SERVICE} job around it, planning {SUBSERVICE_1}, {SUBSERVICE_2} and {SUBSERVICE_3} for shoreline exposure and Maritime winters rather than fair-weather conditions.

## Small town, full coverage

Beyond the fishery, tourism, Acadian culture, ferry services and tidal power round out the local economy, and Digby Neck — the whale-watching peninsula that runs out from the surrounding district — draws visitors through the season. A tourism-facing town keeps its properties presentable, which means steady {SERVICE_CATEGORY} demand from both {PROPERTY_TYPE}. {COMPANY_NAME}, a {BUSINESS_TYPE} with {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years in business, {CERTIFICATIONS} and a {RATING} rating, covers {SERVICE_LIST} across town, and every job gets the same coastal-grade standard whether it faces the water or sits a few streets back.

Digby sits inside Digby District, whose centre is 8.6 km away, and the town serves as the immediate area's hub. Clare's Acadian communities lie 42.5 km down the coast and Annapolis County begins 42.8 km east, so crews working this corner of the province cover a wide territory — but town properties always get {RESPONSE_TIME} response, with {AVAILABILITY} availability when a problem cannot wait for the next open slot.

A town of 2,060 rewards a provider who treats every job as a referral, because neighbours here compare notes. Pricing stays transparent at {PRICE_RANGE} depending on scope, and every project — from a small repair to a full {PROJECT_TYPE} — starts with a {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate and a straight answer about what the work involves. {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} {CTA_PRIMARY}
